title: End-to-End Processing Workshop
narrationScript: This lesson puts the whole method together. Seven stations, one part, and a simple truth. The part remembers everything you do to it, and the indication you read at the end is the sum of every step behind it. Walk the line with me. Station one is preparation. The flaw must be open and empty or nothing downstream matters. Choose the cleaning that fits the soil. Solvent wipe for grease and oil, vapor degreasing or alkaline washing for production lots, ultrasonic for tight geometry. Watch for the mechanical traps. Peening, abrasive blasting, wire brushing, and some machining smear metal across crack mouths and seal them shut. Parts that saw those operations get etched per the procedure before penetrant, or the test is theater. And the part must be dry, because water sitting in a flaw blocks penetrant like a cork. Station two is light and system proof, and it comes before parts, not after. Meter the booth so you know the ultraviolet intensity at the surface meets the minimum, commonly one thousand microwatts per square centimeter, with ambient white light at or below about two footcandles. For visible dye work, verify at least one hundred footcandles at the surface. Then run the daily system check. A known defect comparator panel goes through the same process the parts will see. If the panel cracks show up bright and sharp, the system works today. If they look dim, stop. Find the problem, fix it, prove it, and only then process hardware. Station three is penetrant application and dwell. Spray it, brush it, dip the part, or flow it on, but cover the full examination area and keep it wet for the entire dwell. A typical minimum dwell is ten minutes, longer when the part is cold or when you hunt tight fatigue cracks. The temperature band matters too, commonly forty to one hundred twenty five degrees Fahrenheit for standard work. Penetrant that dries on the part during dwell stops working and creates a removal nightmare, so on a hot day, re-wet or shorten the queue. Station four is removal, the step where most penetrant tests are won or lost. With water washable penetrant, use a coarse spray, commonly at or below forty pounds per square inch, with water in the range of fifty to one hundred degrees Fahrenheit, and watch the surface under ultraviolet light for fluorescent work. Stop when the background is acceptable, not when every last glimmer is gone. Chasing a perfectly black surface strips penetrant out of the flaws, and an over washed part looks beautifully clean and inspects worthless. Solvent removal has its own discipline. Dry wipe first, then a solvent dampened cloth, and never flood the surface. Station five is developer and drying, in the right order. Aqueous developer goes on the wet part, then the dryer. Dry powder and nonaqueous developer go on only after the dryer. Keep the oven gentle, part temperature commonly at or below one hundred sixty degrees Fahrenheit, and no longer than needed. Lay the developer thin and even, then give it its development time, commonly ten minutes minimum. Station six is inspection and evaluation. Read inside the window, under the light you verified at station two, with your eyes dark adapted for fluorescent work. Call the shapes. Continuous linear, intermittent linear, rounded. Sort false from nonrelevant from relevant, document location, size, and type, and pass relevant indications up for evaluation. Station seven is postcleaning and protection. Residue off, part dried, corrosion preventive on when required, paperwork closed. Now the payoff skill, troubleshooting backward. Dim or missing indications point to over washing, penetrant that dried during dwell, a baked part, or a developer coat laid on too thick. A glowing noisy background points to under removal or a rough surface. Fuzzy oversized indications mean the reading came too late. Random bright specks suggest contaminated developer. When any of these shows up, fix the station that caused it, then reprocess the part completely from precleaning forward. There is no spot repair in penetrant testing. One part, seven stations, every step honest, and the indication at the end tells the truth.
imagePrompt: Wide view of a fluorescent penetrant processing line with penetrant dip tank, rinse station, drying oven, developer cabinet, and a darkened inspection booth glowing violet at the far end as a technician carries a basket of parts between stations.

sections.heading: One Part, Seven Stations
sections.body:
This module's final lesson is a workshop: walk one part down the whole line and make every call. The seven stations — pre-clean, lighting and system verification, penetrant application and dwell, removal, developer and drying, inspection and evaluation, postcleaning — are not seven separate skills. They are one chain, and the indication you read at station six is the sum of everything done at stations one through five. The part remembers everything.

Two habits define technicians who run the line well. First, **verify before you process**: light, system check, temperatures, materials — proven good before the first part moves. Second, **trace problems backward**: every bad-looking result at the booth points to a specific upstream station, and the fix happens at that station, never at the symptom. Keep both habits in front of you as we run the line.

sections.caption: The seven-station penetrant line with the typical control values that live at each station.

sections.heading: Stations 1–2: Prepare the Part, Prove the System
sections.body:
**Station 1 — preparation of parts, applied.** The flaw must be open and empty. Match the cleaning to the soil: solvent wipe for local grease and oil, vapor degrease or alkaline wash for production lots, ultrasonic for tight geometry. Then the traps: peening, abrasive blasting, wire brushing, and some machining **smear metal across crack mouths** — a smeared part gets etched per the procedure before penetrant, or it is not really being tested. Acid and chromate residues left in flaws kill fluorescence, so rinse and dry completely after any chemical cleaning. The part enters the line **dry** and inside the working temperature band, commonly 40–125 °F.

**Station 2 — adequate lighting, applied.** Verification comes before parts, not after. Meter the booth: UV-A at the surface at least 1,000 µW/cm², ambient at or below about 2 fc; for visible work, at least 100 fc at the surface. Then run the **daily system performance check**: a known-defect comparator panel — a star-burst or TAM-style panel — goes through the same process the parts will see. Crisp, bright panel indications prove penetrant, removal, developer, and lamp are all working today. Dim or missing panel indications stop the line: find the cause — contaminated or water-laden penetrant, a weak lamp, a wash fault — fix it, re-prove it, and only then process hardware. Parts tested since the last good check become suspect when a panel fails.

sections.heading: Station 3: Penetrant On, Clock Running
sections.body:
**Application of penetrant, applied.** Spray, brush, dip, or flow — coverage is what matters: the entire examination area wetted and **kept wet for the full dwell**. Typical minimum dwell is 10 minutes, and procedures extend it for cold parts and for tight fatigue cracks, because cold penetrant is thicker and slower into a flaw. Stay inside the temperature band, commonly 40–125 °F, and treat its edges with respect — a part at 45 °F may be legal, but it is not behaving like a part at 70 °F.

The classic station-three failure is penetrant **drying on the part** mid-dwell: once dry it stops migrating into flaws and becomes a removal nightmare that drives over-washing. On hot days or warm parts, re-wet during long dwells or shorten the queue so sprayed parts are not waiting in the sun. Log dwell start and end — the times are part of the record, not decoration.

sections.heading: Station 4: Removal — Where Tests Are Won or Lost
sections.body:
**Removal of surface penetrant, applied.** More penetrant tests die at the rinse station than anywhere else. With water-washable penetrant, use a **coarse spray**, typically at or below **40 psi** with water around **50–100 °F**, sweeping at an angle rather than blasting straight in. For fluorescent work, rinse **under UV light** so you can see what is happening. The stopping rule is the whole skill: **stop at an acceptable background, not at zero**. Chasing a perfectly black surface is over-washing — it strips penetrant out of the flaws, and the part comes out looking flawless because the evidence went down the drain. Under-washing fails the other way: a noisy, glowing background that hides discrete indications.

Solvent removal has its own discipline: **dry wipe first** with clean, lint-free cloth, then a **solvent-dampened** cloth for the remainder — never flood or hose the surface with solvent, which dissolves penetrant straight out of the flaws. Post-emulsifiable methods put a timer on the emulsifier: contact time per the procedure, no agitation for lipophilic, concentration checks for hydrophilic. Whatever the method, removal is watched work — eyes on the surface, every part.

sections.heading: Stations 5–7: Develop, Read, Clean
sections.body:
**Station 5 — developer and drying, applied.** Order by form: aqueous developer onto the wet part, then the dryer; dry powder and nonaqueous only after the part is dry. Keep the oven gentle — part temperature commonly at or below 160 °F, time at the minimum — because a baked part gives up its penetrant before the developer can. Film thin and even; development dwell commonly at least 10 minutes from the moment the coating is in place and dry.

**Station 6 — inspection and evaluation, applied.** Read inside the window, under the light proven at station 2, eyes dark-adapted for fluorescent work. Call the shapes — continuous linear, intermittent linear, rounded — separate false from nonrelevant from relevant, and document location, size, character, and the process numbers behind the read. Doubtful indication? The part goes back to station 1 for full reprocessing; there are no touch-ups.

**Station 7 — postcleaning, applied.** Residue off with the mildest effective method, part dried thoroughly including holes and threads, corrosion preventive applied when required, paperwork closed. The line is finished when the hardware leaves inspected, documented, clean, and protected.

Run those seven honestly and the method is close to bulletproof. Shortcut any one and the line keeps moving while quietly finding nothing.

sections.title: No spot repairs
sections.body: Penetrant testing has no patch kit. A missed window, a smeared indication, a doubtful surface, a failed system check — every one of them sends the part back through **complete reprocessing from precleaning forward**. Re-spraying one area gives a result no one can defend, because the process state of that spot is unknown.

sections.caption: Troubleshooting at the booth: trace the symptom back to the station that caused it.
rows: Over-washing; penetrant dried during dwell; part baked in dryer; developer coat too thick
Under-removal, or a surface too rough for the wash to clear
Read past the development window — over-developed
Contaminated dry developer (fluorescent specks); dirty handling
Comparator panel dim on the daily check
Contaminated or diluted penetrant; weak UV lamp; wash fault
2 stop the line and fix the system
Indications that wipe off and never re-bleed
Lint, handling transfer, drainage residue — false indications

sections.caption: Symptom-to-station trace-back: every bad result at the booth points to a specific upstream station, and the part is fully reprocessed after the fix.

figures.title: Trace the symptom back to the station

interactive.title: Run the Line: One Part, Five Decisions
interactive.intro: A machined and shot-peened aluminum fitting arrives for fluorescent penetrant inspection — water-washable Method A with dry powder developer. Walk it down the line and make the calls a Level I makes. Typical values appear throughout; your written procedure governs every number.

steps.prompt: The fitting was shot-peened after machining. Before penetrant goes on, what does the process require?
options: Wipe with solvent and proceed — peening is a surface treatment, not contamination
Etch per the procedure to reopen smeared-over flaws, then clean and dry
Abrasive-blast lightly to roughen the surface so penetrant holds better
steps.feedback: Peening, blasting, and wire brushing smear metal over crack mouths and close the opening capillary action needs. The procedure calls for etching after such operations — and blasting would make the smearing worse, not better.

steps.prompt: Before you process parts, the daily system check on the known-defect comparator panel shows noticeably dimmer indications than the reference. What now?
options: Proceed — production parts are less critical than the panel
Extend penetrant dwell on production parts to compensate
Stop. Find and fix the system problem, re-run the check, and process no parts until it passes
steps.feedback: The comparator panel is the daily proof that the whole system finds flaws. A dim panel means contaminated penetrant, a weak lamp, or a process fault — parts processed on a failing system are worthless results, and parts since the last good check become suspect.

steps.prompt: The shop is cold this morning and the part measures 45 °F. Penetrant dwell is normally ten minutes. The right move is to:
options: Apply penetrant and keep the standard ten-minute dwell
Warm the part toward the middle of the band or extend dwell as the procedure directs — cold penetrant moves slower
Keep the dwell but rinse with hotter water later to even things out
steps.feedback: Forty-five degrees is inside the typical 40–125 °F band, but barely. Cold penetrant is thicker and enters flaws slowly, so procedures direct warming the part or extending dwell. Hotter rinse water fixes nothing and invites over-washing.

steps.prompt: At the rinse station under UV light, most surface glow is gone but a faint background remains in rough pockets. You should:
options: Keep rinsing until every trace of fluorescence is gone
Stop — the target is an acceptable background, not zero fluorescence
Switch to flooding the part with solvent to finish faster
steps.feedback: Rinse to an acceptable background and stop. Chasing a perfectly dark surface over-washes and strips penetrant out of flaws — and flooding with solvent is never a removal technique, least of all for Method A.

steps.prompt: After the dryer you apply dry powder developer and a light film clings on. When does development time start, and when do you read?
options: The clock started back at the rinse station — read immediately
It starts when the powder goes on — read after the minimum, commonly ten minutes, and within the procedure's window
It starts after the part cools completely — read any time the same shift
steps.feedback: For dry developer the development clock starts at application. Wait the minimum, commonly ten minutes, then read inside the window — indications keep spreading, and a read that comes hours late shows fuzzy, oversized bleed-out.

checkQuestions.question: Water-washable fluorescent penetrant with nonaqueous developer: which sequence is correct?
options: Penetrant dwell → rinse → developer → dryer → inspect
Penetrant dwell → rinse → dry the part → apply developer → development dwell → inspect
Penetrant dwell → dry the part → rinse → developer → inspect
Rinse → penetrant dwell → developer → inspect → dry
checkQuestions.explanation: Nonaqueous developer goes only on a completely dry surface, so the part is dried after the rinse and before developer application — then development dwell, then the read.

checkQuestions.question: Parts from one shift show almost no indications, the surfaces look unusually clean and dark under UV, and the morning comparator panel was fine. The first suspect is:
options: Over-washing at the removal station stripping penetrant out of the flaws
Developer applied slightly too thin
UV lamp intensity set too high
Penetrant dwell run slightly longer than the minimum
checkQuestions.explanation: A beautifully dark, ultra-clean background with missing indications is the signature of over-washing — the evidence went down the drain at station 4. The passing morning panel says the materials and lamp were good.

checkQuestions.question: A batch reads with heavy fluorescent background that hides everything. The trace-back points to:
options: Over-development at the developer station
Too much dark adaptation time
Incomplete removal — or a surface too rough for the wash to clear
Postcleaning performed too early
checkQuestions.explanation: A noisy, glowing background is residual surface penetrant: under-removal at station 4, or surface roughness from station 1 that the wash cannot clear. The fix happens there, and the parts are reprocessed.

checkQuestions.question: The booth door seal fails and ambient white light measures about 10 fc during fluorescent inspection. The correct response is to:
options: Continue — UV intensity is unaffected by ambient light
Stop inspecting, restore ambient to about 2 fc or less, re-adapt, and re-read parts viewed under the leak
Double the UV intensity to compensate
Finish the batch in full room light
checkQuestions.explanation: White light washes out fluorescent contrast — at 10 fc, faint indications disappear. The booth condition is restored first, and anything read under the bad condition is read again.

checkQuestions.question: Midway through reading a part, an inspector accidentally smears an indication with a glove. What now?
options: Re-spray developer on that spot and read it again in ten minutes
Record the smear as a rounded indication
Wipe the whole part clean and look again immediately
Stop — the part returns for complete reprocessing from precleaning forward
checkQuestions.explanation: There are no spot repairs in PT. Once an indication is disturbed, the process state of that area is unknown, so the part is cleaned and fully reprocessed to produce a defensible result.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.1–2.7 (liquid penetrant processing, applied practice)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (process sequence and system checks, general reference)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (general reference)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (general reference)
